dependencies.description: SONiC relies on Linux kernel for management plane connectivity, netlink notifications, and some data plane operations.
dependencies.name: Linux kernel networking

dependencies.description: All SONiC services run as Docker containers. Docker engine must be healthy for any service to function.
dependencies.name: Docker engine

dependencies.description: Redis is the IPC backbone. All inter-container communication goes through Redis databases. Redis failure = total system failure.
dependencies.name: Redis

dependencies.description: Data plane forwarding requires a SAI-compatible ASIC with vendor-provided libsai.so. On Cisco 8000-O: Silicon One Q100/Q200 with Cisco SAI.
dependencies.name: SAI-compatible ASIC

failure_modes.description: orchagent calls SAI API to program an ASIC object, SAI returns SAI_STATUS_FAILURE, and orchagent aborts. The swss container restarts, causing temporary traffic disruption while APPL_DB is re-processed.

resolutions.action: Check 'docker logs swss' for the specific SAI error and object type
Check 'docker logs swss' for the specific SAI error and object type
Check 'docker logs swss' for the specific SAI error and object type
Identify the config change that triggered the crash — remove it if possible
Identify the config change that triggered the crash — remove it if possible
Identify the config change that triggered the crash — remove it if possible
Check 'show crm resources all' for resource exhaustion
Check 'show crm resources all' for resource exhaustion
Check 'show crm resources all' for resource exhaustion
If SAI bug suspected: collect 'show techsupport' and check for known issues in SONiC release notes
If SAI bug suspected: collect 'show techsupport' and check for known issues in SONiC release notes
If SAI bug suspected: collect 'show techsupport' and check for known issues in SONiC release notes
Last resort: 'docker restart swss' or cold reboot to reset ASIC state
Last resort: 'docker restart swss' or cold reboot to reset ASIC state
Last resort: 'docker restart swss' or cold reboot to reset ASIC state

root_causes.cause: SAI programming error for unsupported configuration (e.g., ECMP hash offset on certain platforms)
ASIC resource exhaustion — table full when orchagent tries to create new entry
SAI library bug in vendor libsai.so — specific object/attribute combination triggers error
Stale ASIC state after failed warm reboot — SAI objects in unexpected state

failure_modes.scenario: orchagent crash on SAI programming failure
symptoms: swss container in 'Restarting' state in 'docker ps -a'
Syslog shows 'orchagent: Exiting with code ...'
'docker logs swss' shows SAI error before crash
Routes/neighbors temporarily missing from hardware

failure_modes.description: After warm reboot, syncd tries to reconcile the saved ASIC state with the current ASIC state. SAI comparison/remove operations fail, syncd signals orchagent to abort. Warm reboot fails and may fall back to cold reboot.

resolutions.action: Check 'docker logs syncd' for the specific SAI object type and operation that failed
Check 'docker logs syncd' for the specific SAI object type and operation that failed
Check 'docker logs syncd' for the specific SAI object type and operation that failed
If CONFIG_DB was changed during warm reboot: do not modify CONFIG_DB during warm reboot window
If CONFIG_DB was changed during warm reboot: do not modify CONFIG_DB during warm reboot window
If CONFIG_DB was changed during warm reboot: do not modify CONFIG_DB during warm reboot window
If version upgrade: verify db_migrator.py ran successfully, check release notes for known warm reboot issues
If version upgrade: verify db_migrator.py ran successfully, check release notes for known warm reboot issues
If version upgrade: verify db_migrator.py ran successfully, check release notes for known warm reboot issues
If persistent: cold reboot to reset ASIC state, then re-validate warm reboot on next maintenance window
If persistent: cold reboot to reset ASIC state, then re-validate warm reboot on next maintenance window
If persistent: cold reboot to reset ASIC state, then re-validate warm reboot on next maintenance window
Collect 'show techsupport' BEFORE cold reboot for TAC analysis
Collect 'show techsupport' BEFORE cold reboot for TAC analysis
Collect 'show techsupport' BEFORE cold reboot for TAC analysis

root_causes.cause: ASIC state changed between warm shutdown and warm restart (race condition)
SAI object deletion fails due to dependency ordering issue in vendor SAI library
CONFIG_DB was modified during the warm reboot window
SONiC version upgrade changed SAI object schema — db_migrator.py didn't handle the change

failure_modes.description: During warm reboot, FRR bgpd takes longer to restart and reconcile than the configured GR timer. Peers withdraw all routes learned from this device, causing full routing reconvergence and potential traffic black-holing.

resolutions.action: Increase BGP GR restart-time: vtysh -> 'bgp graceful-restart restart-time 360' (or higher for large tables)
Increase BGP GR restart-time: vtysh -> 'bgp graceful-restart restart-time 360' (or higher for large tables)
Increase BGP GR restart-time: vtysh -> 'bgp graceful-restart restart-time 360' (or higher for large tables)
Verify warm reboot timing in lab before production: 'sudo warm-reboot' and measure total time
Verify warm reboot timing in lab before production: 'sudo warm-reboot' and measure total time
Verify warm reboot timing in lab before production: 'sudo warm-reboot' and measure total time
Pre-warm boot check: ensure CONFIG_DB is saved and no pending changes
Pre-warm boot check: ensure CONFIG_DB is saved and no pending changes
Pre-warm boot check: ensure CONFIG_DB is saved and no pending changes
Consider fast-reboot instead of warm-reboot if GR timing cannot be guaranteed
Consider fast-reboot instead of warm-reboot if GR timing cannot be guaranteed
Consider fast-reboot instead of warm-reboot if GR timing cannot be guaranteed

root_causes.cause: BGP GR restart-time too low (default 120s) for the route table size
FRR bgpd container takes too long to start (image loading, CPU contention)
fpmsyncd reconciliation stalls, delaying route re-advertisement
CONFIG_DB has large BGP configuration that takes time to process

failure_modes.scenario: BGP Graceful Restart timer expiry during warm reboot
symptoms: All BGP sessions reset after warm reboot
'show ip bgp summary' shows all peers recently reset (uptime < warm reboot duration)
Traffic black-hole for routes that were only via this device
Peer routers show 'BGP GR timer expired' in their logs

failure_modes.description: During SONiC version upgrade, db_migrator.py fails to migrate CONFIG_DB schema from the old version format to the new version format. Device boots with broken or incompatible configuration.

resolutions.action: Check /var/log/syslog for db_migrator error messages
Check /var/log/syslog for db_migrator error messages
Check /var/log/syslog for db_migrator error messages
If partial migration: manually fix CONFIG_DB entries to match new schema (reference sonic-utilities source)
If partial migration: manually fix CONFIG_DB entries to match new schema (reference sonic-utilities source)
If partial migration: manually fix CONFIG_DB entries to match new schema (reference sonic-utilities source)
If migration completely failed: restore backup config_db.json from pre-upgrade and reapply config
If migration completely failed: restore backup config_db.json from pre-upgrade and reapply config
If migration completely failed: restore backup config_db.json from pre-upgrade and reapply config
For multi-version jumps: upgrade incrementally (e.g., 202211 -> 202311 -> 202405 -> 202411)
For multi-version jumps: upgrade incrementally (e.g., 202211 -> 202311 -> 202405 -> 202411)
For multi-version jumps: upgrade incrementally (e.g., 202211 -> 202311 -> 202405 -> 202411)
Collect 'sonic-cfggen -d --print-data' output for comparison with expected schema
Collect 'sonic-cfggen -d --print-data' output for comparison with expected schema
Collect 'sonic-cfggen -d --print-data' output for comparison with expected schema

root_causes.cause: db_migrator.py does not have migration path for the specific version jump
Custom or non-standard CONFIG_DB entries that db_migrator doesn't handle
Interrupted upgrade process left CONFIG_DB in half-migrated state
Multi-version jump (e.g., 202211 -> 202411) may have compounding migration issues

failure_modes.scenario: CONFIG_DB schema migration failure on upgrade
symptoms: Services fail to start after image upgrade
Syslog shows db_migrator errors
CONFIG_DB has tables/keys in old format that new *mgrd daemons don't recognize
Some features work, others don't (partial migration)

failure_modes.description: A Docker container repeatedly crashes and restarts. Docker applies exponential backoff between restarts. The service is effectively down with brief periods of operation between crashes.

resolutions.action: Identify the crashing container: 'docker ps -a'
Identify the crashing container: 'docker ps -a'
Identify the crashing container: 'docker ps -a'
Check crash logs: 'docker logs <container>' — look for the last error before exit
Check crash logs: 'docker logs <container>' — look for the last error before exit
Check crash logs: 'docker logs <container>' — look for the last error before exit
If OOM: check memory with 'docker stats <container>' and system 'free -m'
If OOM: check memory with 'docker stats <container>' and system 'free -m'
If OOM: check memory with 'docker stats <container>' and system 'free -m'
If config-triggered: identify the bad CONFIG_DB entry and remove it
If config-triggered: identify the bad CONFIG_DB entry and remove it
If config-triggered: identify the bad CONFIG_DB entry and remove it
If software bug: check SONiC release notes for known issues, consider image rollback
If software bug: check SONiC release notes for known issues, consider image rollback
If software bug: check SONiC release notes for known issues, consider image rollback
Emergency: 'sonic-installer set-default <previous-image>' and reboot to rollback
Emergency: 'sonic-installer set-default <previous-image>' and reboot to rollback
Emergency: 'sonic-installer set-default <previous-image>' and reboot to rollback

root_causes.cause: Software bug triggered by specific configuration or state (segfault, assertion failure)
Memory exhaustion (OOM killer terminates container process)
Dependency failure — container depends on another service that's not ready
Corrupted container image or filesystem
CONFIG_DB has invalid entries that crash the daemon during startup parsing

symptoms: 'docker ps -a' shows container status as 'Restarting (1) X seconds ago'
Multiple restarts visible in 'docker ps -a' — restart count incrementing
Intermittent service availability (works for seconds, then crashes)
Syslog flooded with container start/stop messages

failure_modes.description: The configuration in CONFIG_DB does not match what is actually programmed in the ASIC (via ASIC_DB). Routes, ACLs, or interfaces may exist in config but not in hardware, or vice versa. Traffic behavior doesn't match configured policy.

resolutions.action: Compare CONFIG_DB with APPL_DB and ASIC_DB for the affected feature
Compare CONFIG_DB with APPL_DB and ASIC_DB for the affected feature
Compare CONFIG_DB with APPL_DB and ASIC_DB for the affected feature
'config reload' to force full resync from CONFIG_DB through the entire pipeline
'config reload' to force full resync from CONFIG_DB through the entire pipeline
'config reload' to force full resync from CONFIG_DB through the entire pipeline
If specific entries missing: remove and re-add the config to trigger reprogramming
If specific entries missing: remove and re-add the config to trigger reprogramming
If specific entries missing: remove and re-add the config to trigger reprogramming
If widespread divergence: cold reboot to reset all state
If widespread divergence: cold reboot to reset all state
If widespread divergence: cold reboot to reset all state
Check 'docker logs swss' for SAI errors that indicate which entries failed to program
Check 'docker logs swss' for SAI errors that indicate which entries failed to program
Check 'docker logs swss' for SAI errors that indicate which entries failed to program

root_causes.cause: orchagent crashed during programming and missed entries when it restarted
SAI returned error for specific entries but orchagent continued processing others
Warm reboot reconciliation was incomplete — some objects not re-programmed
'config load' applied partial changes that weren't fully processed by *mgrd daemons
Race condition: config change arrived while orchagent was still processing previous batch

symptoms: Config shows interface up but traffic not forwarding
ACL configured but not filtering traffic
Route in 'show ip route' but not being forwarded in hardware
'show crm resources' doesn't match expected counts based on config

failure_modes.description: ASIC resources (FIB routes, ACL entries, next-hops, FDB entries, ECMP groups) are exhausted. New entries silently fail to program in hardware. Control plane (FRR, CONFIG_DB) shows the entries exist, but they are not in the data plane.

resolutions.action: Identify exhausted resource: 'show crm resources all'
Identify exhausted resource: 'show crm resources all'
Identify exhausted resource: 'show crm resources all'
For FIB: filter routes at BGP (prefix-lists, ORF), reduce full table to partial/default
For FIB: filter routes at BGP (prefix-lists, ORF), reduce full table to partial/default
For FIB: filter routes at BGP (prefix-lists, ORF), reduce full table to partial/default
For ACL: consolidate ACL entries, use fewer but broader rules
For ACL: consolidate ACL entries, use fewer but broader rules
For ACL: consolidate ACL entries, use fewer but broader rules
For FDB: check for MAC learning loops, reduce broadcast domains, implement storm-control
For FDB: check for MAC learning loops, reduce broadcast domains, implement storm-control
For FDB: check for MAC learning loops, reduce broadcast domains, implement storm-control
For ECMP: reduce max-paths or use fewer equal-cost paths
For ECMP: reduce max-paths or use fewer equal-cost paths
For ECMP: reduce max-paths or use fewer equal-cost paths
Configure CRM thresholds for proactive alerting: CONFIG_DB CRM table
Configure CRM thresholds for proactive alerting: CONFIG_DB CRM table
Configure CRM thresholds for proactive alerting: CONFIG_DB CRM table

root_causes.cause: Route table exceeds ASIC FIB capacity (Q100: ~2M IPv4, Q200: 2-3M IPv4)
ACL TCAM exhausted by too many ACL entries
FDB table full — MAC learning loops or too many endpoints
ECMP group table full — too many ECMP paths
hw-module profile not optimized for the deployment's scale pattern

failure_modes.scenario: CRM resource exhaustion — silent programming failures
symptoms: 'show crm resources all' shows Available Count at or near 0 for a resource
New routes learned by BGP but not forwarded in hardware (black-hole)
New ACL rules configured but not enforced
New MAC addresses not learned (flooding persists)
'docker logs swss' may show SAI_STATUS_TABLE_FULL errors

failure_modes.description: The persisted configuration file (/etc/sonic/config_db.json) is corrupted or contains invalid JSON. Device boots with broken or partial configuration, or fails to start services.

resolutions.action: Check file syntax: 'python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('/etc/sonic/config_db.json'))"'
Check file syntax: 'python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('/etc/sonic/config_db.json'))"'
Check file syntax: 'python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('/etc/sonic/config_db.json'))"'
If syntax error: fix the JSON manually or restore from backup
If syntax error: fix the JSON manually or restore from backup
If syntax error: fix the JSON manually or restore from backup
If no backup: 'sonic-cfggen -H -k <hwsku> --print-data' generates a minimal default config
If no backup: 'sonic-cfggen -H -k <hwsku> --print-data' generates a minimal default config
If no backup: 'sonic-cfggen -H -k <hwsku> --print-data' generates a minimal default config
After fixing: 'config reload config_db.json' to apply
After fixing: 'config reload config_db.json' to apply
After fixing: 'config reload config_db.json' to apply
Prevention: always use 'config save' command, never edit config_db.json manually
Prevention: always use 'config save' command, never edit config_db.json manually
Prevention: always use 'config save' command, never edit config_db.json manually

root_causes.cause: Manual edit of config_db.json with syntax error (missing comma, bracket, quote)
'config save' interrupted (power loss, kill signal) leaving partial write
Disk full when 'config save' was writing
File permissions changed preventing sonic-cfggen from reading

symptoms: Services fail to start after reboot
Syslog shows JSON parse errors from sonic-cfggen
'sonic-cfggen -d --print-data' produces errors or empty output
Some interfaces/features missing after reboot

failure_modes.description: Engineer uses 'config load' expecting full config replacement (like 'config reload') or uses 'config reload' not understanding it restarts all services. 'config load' only updates CONFIG_DB keys from a file — it does NOT restart services or remove keys not in the file. 'config reload' restarts everything.

resolutions.action: For full config replacement: use 'config reload <file>' (disruptive, restarts all services)
For full config replacement: use 'config reload <file>' (disruptive, restarts all services)
For full config replacement: use 'config reload <file>' (disruptive, restarts all services)
For incremental updates: use 'config load <file>' then restart specific services if needed
For incremental updates: use 'config load <file>' then restart specific services if needed
For incremental updates: use 'config load <file>' then restart specific services if needed
To remove entries: use specific 'config' CLI commands to delete, or 'config reload' for full reset
To remove entries: use specific 'config' CLI commands to delete, or 'config reload' for full reset
To remove entries: use specific 'config' CLI commands to delete, or 'config reload' for full reset
Always 'config save' after validating changes
Always 'config save' after validating changes
Always 'config save' after validating changes

root_causes.cause: Using 'config load' when full config replacement was intended (should use 'config reload')
Using 'config reload' in production without understanding it's disruptive (restarts all services)
Expecting 'config load' to delete keys not in the loaded file (it only adds/updates, never deletes)

failure_modes.scenario: config reload vs config load misuse
symptoms: Config shows correctly in CONFIG_DB but feature not working (config load didn't trigger *mgrd)
Services unexpectedly restart (unintended config reload)
Old config entries persist even after loading new config (config load doesn't delete missing keys)
Partial config apply — some features work, others don't

failure_modes.description: The database (Redis) container crashes or becomes unresponsive. Since ALL inter-container communication goes through Redis, this is equivalent to a total system failure. No container can read or write state.

resolutions.action: If database container is restarting: 'docker logs database' for root cause
If database container is restarting: 'docker logs database' for root cause
If database container is restarting: 'docker logs database' for root cause
If OOM: check 'redis-cli INFO memory' (if accessible), check system memory with 'free -m'
If OOM: check 'redis-cli INFO memory' (if accessible), check system memory with 'free -m'
If OOM: check 'redis-cli INFO memory' (if accessible), check system memory with 'free -m'
If disk full: clean up /var/dump/ (old techsupport bundles), /var/core/ (old core dumps)
If disk full: clean up /var/dump/ (old techsupport bundles), /var/core/ (old core dumps)
If disk full: clean up /var/dump/ (old techsupport bundles), /var/core/ (old core dumps)
Last resort: cold reboot — database container failure usually requires full restart
Last resort: cold reboot — database container failure usually requires full restart
Last resort: cold reboot — database container failure usually requires full restart
Prevention: monitor Redis memory usage, set maxmemory policy, clean old dump files
Prevention: monitor Redis memory usage, set maxmemory policy, clean old dump files

root_causes.cause: Redis OOM — too much data in Redis databases exceeds memory limit
Redis persistence (RDB/AOF) failure due to disk space
Kernel OOM killer targets Redis process
Redis bug triggered by specific key pattern

symptoms: 'docker ps -a' shows database container exited or restarting
ALL show commands fail or return empty data
Multiple containers may enter restart loops (dependency on Redis)
Complete loss of management plane

resolutions.action: Check 'docker logs bgp --tail 200' for the crash signature (stack trace, assertion)

failure_modes.description: During warm/fast reboot, services start in wrong order due to systemd dependency misconfiguration. A service that depends on another starts before its dependency is ready, causing it to fail or behave incorrectly.

resolutions.action: Check 'show warm_restart table' for stuck services
Check 'show warm_restart table' for stuck services

Restart the stuck service: 'docker restart <container>'
Restart the stuck service: 'docker restart <container>'

If reproducible: check /etc/systemd/system/ for service unit dependency declarations

root_causes.cause: systemd service unit with wrong After=/Requires= dependencies
Docker compose ordering doesn't guarantee Redis is accepting connections when dependent containers start
Custom feature container added without proper dependency declaration
Timing race — dependency starts but isn't ready to accept connections yet

symptoms: Warm reboot completes but some features don't work
Container logs show 'connection refused' to Redis or dependency service
'show warm_restart table' shows some services stuck in 'initialized' state
Service works after manual 'docker restart <container>'

messages.description: SAI API call completed successfully. ASIC programmed as requested.
messages.name: SAI_STATUS_SUCCESS
messages.when_sent: After every successful SAI operation (create, set, remove, get)

messages.description: Generic SAI API failure. ASIC could not process the request.
messages.name: SAI_STATUS_FAILURE
messages.when_sent: When SAI library encounters an error programming the ASIC

messages.description: ASIC table (FIB, ACL, FDB) is full. Cannot program new entries.
messages.name: SAI_STATUS_TABLE_FULL
messages.when_sent: When orchagent tries to create a new SAI object and the corresponding ASIC table is at capacity

messages.description: Requested SAI operation is not supported by this ASIC/SAI implementation.
messages.name: SAI_STATUS_NOT_SUPPORTED
messages.when_sent: When orchagent requests a feature the ASIC cannot implement

messages.description: Redis keyspace notification that a CONFIG_DB table has been modified.
messages.name: CONFIG_DB_NOTIFICATION
messages.when_sent: When any config change is written to CONFIG_DB (via CLI, REST, gNMI)

purpose: SONiC container architecture, Redis database infrastructure, SAI abstraction layer, and warm/fast reboot mechanisms. NOT a network protocol — this is the SONiC-specific infrastructure that all protocols depend on.

states.description: All Docker containers running, Redis databases responsive, orchagent processing normally, syncd programming ASIC via SAI. Normal operational state.
states.name: HEALTHY

states.description: One or more Docker containers have exited or are in a restart loop. Impact depends on which container: database = total outage, swss/syncd = data plane impact, bgp = routing impact, pmon = monitoring blind.
states.name: CONTAINER_DOWN

states.description: orchagent is running but not processing APPL_DB entries. New routes, neighbors, and config changes are not being programmed into ASIC_DB. Often occurs during warm restart reconciliation.
states.name: ORCHAGENT_STUCK

states.description: syncd cannot communicate with the ASIC via SAI. ASIC_DB entries are not being translated to hardware programming. Data plane stale or broken.
states.name: SYNCD_FAILURE

states.description: Device is in the warm reboot window. Data plane preserved from pre-reboot state while control plane restarts and reconciles. BGP Graceful Restart active.
states.name: WARM_REBOOT_IN_PROGRESS

states.description: Warm reboot did not complete successfully. May have fallen back to cold reboot, or services may be stuck in reconciliation. Traffic impact likely.
states.name: WARM_REBOOT_FAILED

states.description: Redis databases are inconsistent with each other (CONFIG_DB vs APPL_DB vs ASIC_DB). Config changes may not be reflected in hardware, or hardware state may not match config.
states.name: DB_INCONSISTENT

states.description: One or more ASIC resources (FIB, ACL, next-hop, FDB) are at or near capacity. New entries silently fail to program. CRM thresholds exceeded.
states.name: CRM_EXHAUSTED

timers.name: BGP Graceful Restart timer
timers.on_expiry: Peers withdraw all routes learned from the SONiC device. Total routing reconvergence event.

os_defaults.default: 120s default in FRR, often insufficient for large-scale reconciliation. Increase to 240-360s for large route tables.
os_defaults.notes: The #1 warm reboot failure cause. Set via vtysh: 'bgp graceful-restart restart-time <seconds>'.

timers.name: Docker container restart backoff
timers.on_expiry: Docker attempts to restart the crashed container. After repeated failures, backoff increases.

timers.name: orchagent warm restart reconciliation timeout
timers.on_expiry: orchagent declares warm restart reconciliation failed. Triggers fallback to cold restart behavior.

os_defaults.default: 300s hardcoded in orchagent source. Watchdog monitors for stuck state >60s.
os_defaults.notes: If reconciliation exceeds this, swss container may exit. Check 'show warm_restart table' for stuck state.

timers.name: CRM polling interval
timers.on_expiry: CRM re-checks ASIC resource utilization and updates CRM counters in COUNTERS_DB.

os_defaults.default: 300s default. Config: 'crm config polling interval <seconds>' in CONFIG_DB CRM table.
os_defaults.notes: At default 5-minute interval, resource exhaustion can go undetected for minutes. Reduce to 60s in production.

timers.name: flex counter polling interval
timers.on_expiry: syncd polls ASIC counter registers and updates COUNTERS_DB.

os_defaults.default: Default varies by counter type: port=1000ms, queue=10000ms, watermark=10000ms.
os_defaults.notes: Config: 'counterpoll <type> interval <ms>'. Aggressive polling increases CPU load on syncd.

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Check docker logs for the failing container. Common: orchagent segfault, syncd SAI error, bgp (FRR) OOM.
transitions.trigger: Container crash (OOM, segfault, unhandled exception), manual stop, or systemd dependency failure

transitions.failure_if_stuck: If container is in restart loop, check 'docker logs <name>' for the root cause error. May need image upgrade or config fix.
transitions.trigger: Container auto-restart by Docker/systemd, or manual 'docker restart <name>'
All services complete reconciliation within GR timer window

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Check 'show warm_restart table' and 'docker logs swss'. May need cold reboot to recover.
transitions.trigger: BGP GR timer expires, orchagent reconciliation fails, CONFIG_DB migration fails, or service dependency race

transitions.failure_if_stuck: 'docker restart swss' to restart orchagent. Check docker logs swss for the specific error.
transitions.trigger: orchagent encounters unresolvable SAI error, memory exhaustion, or warm restart reconciliation hang

transitions.failure_if_stuck: 'docker restart syncd'. If persistent, check SAI/SDK version compatibility and ASIC health.
transitions.trigger: SAI API returns persistent errors, ASIC communication failure, or syncd crash

transitions.failure_if_stuck: 'config reload' to resync all databases from CONFIG_DB. Nuclear option: cold reboot.
transitions.trigger: Partial config apply failure, interrupted 'config reload', warm reboot with CONFIG_DB change, or orchagent crash during programming

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Reduce scale (filter routes, consolidate ACLs) or use hw-module profile for different resource allocation (Silicon One).
transitions.trigger: Route/ACL/FDB scale exceeds ASIC capacity. Gradual resource consumption or sudden route injection.

transport: Docker containers + Redis IPC + SAI API

notes.note: This protocol entry covers SONiC infrastructure (containers, Redis, SAI, warm reboot) — not a specific network protocol. Every other protocol on SONiC depends on this infrastructure being healthy. Always check container health ('docker ps -a') and Redis connectivity ('redis-cli ping') before debugging individual protocol issues.
